us-politics.headline: Trump's Iran Threats Divide Parties and His Own Allies
us-politics.body:
Trump's Easter message threatening Iran over nuclear talks drew sharp condemnation from both sides of the aisle. Sen. Chris Murphy called the threat a potential war crime, while Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene broke with the president, calling his posture "insane." Iran separately confirmed a US aircraft was destroyed during a recovery mission for a missing service member - a mission Netanyahu publicly praised Trump for.

House Republicans are pushing back on the idea of ground troops to open the Strait of Hormuz, with at least one member saying it won't be necessary. Rep. Madeleine Dean questioned the administration's broader strategy on Iran and its proposed budget priorities. The episode exposed rare fractures within the GOP on foreign policy.

sources.title: Chris Murphy on Trump’s Iran threat: ‘A clear war crime’
Iran says US aircraft destroyed during recovery mission for missing service member
Rep. Madeleine Dean discusses Iran and President Trump's proposed budget
House Republican says he doesn’t think ground troops will be needed to open the Strait of Hormuz
Marjorie Taylor Greene rails against ‘insane’ Trump after Easter Iran threat
Netanyahu says he spoke with Trump, congratulated him on rescuing downed pilot

us-politics.headline: Trump's Mail-In Voting Order Faces Legal and Political Pushback
us-politics.body:
Stacey Abrams called Trump's executive order targeting mail-in voting "patently illegal," arguing it oversteps presidential authority on election administration. The order has reignited debate over voting access ahead of future election cycles, with Democrats framing it as voter suppression and Republicans arguing it targets fraud.

The dispute reflects a broader fight over election rules that has persisted since 2020. Critics say the order would disproportionately affect lower-income and elderly voters who rely on mail ballots. The administration has not publicly responded to the legal challenges.

sources.title: Stacey Abrams on Trump’s mail-in voting order: ‘Patently illegal’

us-politics.headline: GOP Struggles to Reopen DHS as Internal Divisions Deepen
us-politics.body:
Republican efforts to reopen the Department of Homeland Security have stalled, with divisions among House members and a lack of direct engagement from Trump complicating negotiations. Speaker Johnson and Senate Majority Leader Thune have struggled to align their caucuses on a path forward.

The impasse reflects wider tensions inside the GOP over spending and government operations. Democrats have largely stayed on the sidelines, watching Republicans manage the fallout. No clear timeline for resolution has emerged.

sources.title: Republican divisions, Trump’s detachment stymie GOP efforts to reopen DHS

us-politics.headline: Democrats Debate Cuba Policy and 2028 Political Strategy
us-politics.body:
Two Democratic lawmakers visited Cuba and publicly condemned US sanctions, calling them an "economic bombing" and a "blockade of fuel." The remarks put them at odds with the party mainstream and drew criticism from Republicans who accused them of siding with an authoritarian government.

Separately, Rep. Ruben Gallego said he is open to a 2028 presidential run, and Rep. Ro Khanna argued Democrats must engage with non-traditional media figures like Hasan Piker to win future elections. The comments signal an ongoing internal debate about the party's direction, tone, and coalition-building strategy after 2024.

sources.title: Pair of Democrat lawmakers slam 'blockade of fuel' to Cuba, 'economic bombing' after visit to island
Khanna: Those saying ‘don’t engage’ with personalities like Hasan Piker ‘will cost us future elections’
Gallego on possible 2028 presidential run: ‘We have to look at it’

au-politics.headline: Fuel Crisis Bites as Australia Scrambles to Secure Supply
au-politics.body:
Australia's fuel crisis is reshaping daily life and political debate. The government says shipments are secured well into May, but the disruption has already pushed used electric vehicle prices higher as drivers ditch combustion engines. Demand for secondhand EVs is spiking sharply.

From the right, the Spectator argues Australia has failed to replicate the US model of guaranteed domestic oil supply, framing the crisis as a policy failure years in the making. The government has not yet addressed that structural criticism publicly. The auction clearance rate also hit its lowest point since 2022 this weekend, adding to a picture of economic pressure on households already stretched by rising living costs.

sources.title: National auction clearance rate hits lowest point since 2022 – as it happened
Used EV prices rise as Australia’s fuel crisis hits: ‘Doesn’t make sense to hold on to a combustion engine’
The US guaranteed its oil, why can’t Australia?
Australia's fuel shipments secured 'well into' May

au-politics.headline: Free Train Tickets Backfire as Regional Victoria Passengers Left Standing
au-politics.body:
Victoria's free public transport offer over the Easter long weekend overwhelmed regional train services, forcing passengers to stand for hours on crowded carriages. Demand far exceeded capacity on key routes, drawing criticism of the government's planning ahead of the promotion.

The policy was designed to ease road congestion and encourage public transport use during a peak travel period. Instead it exposed the limits of regional rail infrastructure. No government response to the overcrowding complaints had been issued at time of reporting.

sources.title: Passengers forced to stand for hours on regional Victoria trains as free tickets supercharge long weekend demand

au-politics.headline: ABC Privatisation and Social Media Laws Spark Conservative Debate
au-politics.body:
Two pieces in the Spectator Australia signal growing tension on the right over media and free speech policy. One argues the ABC should be privatised, offering a valuation of the public broadcaster and questioning why taxpayers should fund it. The other attacks Liberal Party support for social media censorship laws, calling it a strategic and philosophical mistake.

Both pieces reflect a broader conservative frustration with the direction of Coalition policy heading into the post-election period. The ABC has not responded publicly to the privatisation argument. The social media debate follows the government's under-16 ban legislation, which passed with bipartisan support late last year.

sources.title: Should the ABC be privatised? A modest valuation…
Liberals are fools to support social media censorship

au-politics.headline: Outback Flood Death and Easter Weekend Incidents Mark Grim Holiday
au-politics.body:
A man's body was recovered near Innamincka in South Australia's far north after he was swept away attempting to cross floodwaters on foot. The death is one of several serious incidents over the Easter break. A ski boat launched into a tree during the Mildura 100 race on the Murray River, sending four people to hospital.

A 16-year-old surfer in South Australia survived a shark attack, describing himself as lucky to still have his foot. A body was also found after a house fire in Devonport, Tasmania. Emergency services across multiple states faced a heavy workload across the long weekend.

sources.title: Man’s body found after being swept away in outback flood waters
Murray River ski boat launches into tree as race puts four in hospital
16yo surfer feels 'lucky' to have foot after SA shark attack
Man found dead after being swept away in floodwaters in SA's far north
Body found after house fire in north-west Tasmania

world.headline: Iran War Escalates as Trump Claims Victory, Hormuz Tensions Mount
world.body:
The US rescued a downed American airman from a remote area of Iran, prompting Trump to declare victory on social media in characteristically combative terms. Tehran rejected a US deadline to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and fighting near the waterway has reporters describing conditions at the edge of a battlefield. Two people were killed in Haifa amid ongoing exchanges.

The conflict is reshaping the wider region. Analysts are debating a new post-war order for the Strait of Hormuz, while Lebanon continues to absorb waves of displaced civilians. In the UK, seven protesters were arrested near an RAF base used by US forces, reflecting growing domestic opposition to the operation.

sources.title: A new regional order for the Strait of Hormuz
Trump declares victory after rescue but threats to US operation in Iran still loom
Iran war live: Tehran rejects Trump’s Hormuz deadline; 2 killed in Haifa
'We are at the edge of a battlefield': BBC reports near Strait of Hormuz
How rescue of US airman in remote part of Iran unfolded
UK police arrest seven protesters near RAF base used by US
Trump threatens Iran in expletive-filled social media post

world.headline: Gaza Strikes Continue as Hamas Refuses Disarmament Demands
world.body:
Israel carried out fresh air strikes on Gaza City, hitting a crowd in one of the attacks, while simultaneously striking targets in Iran and Lebanon. Hamas's armed wing flatly rejected disarmament as a condition for any deal, hardening a standoff that has kept negotiations stalled. The humanitarian situation inside Gaza remains severe and largely unaddressed by international actors.

The parallel fronts are stretching Israeli military operations across multiple theatres simultaneously. Critics argue the Gaza crisis has been systematically sidelined by the wider Iran conflict, leaving civilians with dwindling access to aid.

sources.title: Has the humanitarian crisis in Gaza been ignored?
Hamas armed wing says disarmament demands not acceptable
Israeli air strike hits crowd in Gaza City
Hamas armed wing rejects disarmament while Israel continues attacks
Israel continues deadly attacks on Gaza while striking Iran, Lebanon

world.headline: Iran War's Economic Fallout Hits Workers From Vietnam to Nigeria
world.body:
The conflict's shockwaves are reaching far beyond the Middle East. In Vietnam, gig workers - motorcycle taxi and delivery drivers - are being squeezed by fuel price spikes tied directly to the disruption of Gulf oil flows. The cost surge is hitting low-income workers with little buffer.

Elsewhere, Nigeria's army rescued 31 hostages taken during a church attack, a reminder that regional instability compounds existing security crises. In Europe, Hungary alleged a plot to blow up a gas pipeline days before a national election, a claim that will sharpen energy security anxieties already elevated by the Iran war.

sources.title: Vietnam’s gig workers slammed by rising fuel costs amid fallout of Iran war
Nigeria's army rescues 31 hostages after church attack
Hungary alleges plot to blow up gas pipeline ahead of election

world.headline: Russians in Exile Grieve as Ukraine Deepens Syria Security Ties
world.body:
Russians living abroad are navigating a particular kind of loss - cut off from home, unable to mourn publicly or return safely, and caught between guilt and grief as the war grinds on. DW spoke to exiles grappling with the psychological weight of displacement and estrangement from family.

Meanwhile, Ukraine is expanding its diplomatic footprint. President Zelenskyy announced a security cooperation agreement with Syria, with both countries agreeing to exchange military and intelligence experience. The deal signals Kyiv's effort to build alliances beyond its immediate neighbourhood.

sources.title: Russians living in exile cope with grief far from home
Ukraine and Syria to cooperate on security, Zelenskyy says

ai.headline: Microsoft Labels Copilot 'Entertainment Only' in Fine Print
ai.body:
Microsoft's terms of service now classify Copilot as being "for entertainment purposes only," a designation that effectively strips users of any expectation of reliability or accuracy. The language, buried in the ToS, raises serious questions about how seriously Microsoft stands behind its flagship AI product at a time when enterprises are betting workflows on it.

The framing is a legal shield, not a product philosophy - but the gap between Microsoft's marketing and its liability disclaimers is hard to ignore. Copilot is sold as a productivity tool. Calling it entertainment in the fine print suggests the company wants the revenue without the accountability.

sources.title: Copilot is 'for entertainment purposes only', per Microsoft's terms of use
Copilot is ‘for entertainment purposes only,’ according to Microsoft’s terms of use

ai.headline: Gemma 4 Runs Locally on iPhones and MacBooks
ai.body:
Google's Gemma 4 model is now running on consumer hardware, with users deploying it on iPhones via the Google AI Edge Gallery app and on Apple Silicon Macs through LM Studio's new headless CLI. Developers are also pairing it with Claude Code for local workflows, cutting out cloud dependencies entirely.

Separately, a Show HN project called Parlor demonstrates real-time audio and video input with voice output running Gemma on an M3 Pro. Another project, Gemma Gem, embeds the model directly in a browser with no API keys required. The week marks a notable shift in how capable local AI inference has become on mainstream devices.

sources.title: Gemma 4 on iPhone
Running Gemma 4 locally with LM Studio's new headless CLI and Claude Code
Show HN: Real-time AI (audio/video in, voice out) on an M3 Pro with Gemma E2B
Show HN: Gemma Gem – AI model embedded in a browser – no API keys, no cloud

ai.headline: Japan's Robots Fill Jobs, Not Replace Workers
ai.body:
Japan is deploying physical AI robots not to displace workers but to fill roles that go unfilled due to an aging population and chronic labor shortages. The country is proving that experimental robotics can function in real-world conditions - warehouses, care facilities, service roles - where Western deployments have mostly stalled in pilots.

The distinction matters. Japan's framing of robots as gap-fillers rather than replacements reflects a demographic reality: there simply aren't enough workers. That context shapes public acceptance and regulatory tolerance in ways that make Japan a more useful test case for physical AI than most markets.

sources.title: In Japan, the robot isn't coming for your job; it's filling the one nobody wants
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants

ai.headline: Employers Mine Personal Data to Suppress Your Salary
ai.body:
Employers are using personal data - spending habits, location history, financial signals - to estimate the lowest salary a candidate will accept before making an offer. The practice turns the negotiation into an asymmetric information game where the employer holds the cards.

The tactic is a logical extension of the same data-brokerage industry that powers targeted advertising, now applied to labor markets. Workers rarely know it is happening. The result is that two candidates with identical qualifications may receive meaningfully different offers based on what their data profile reveals about their financial pressure.

sources.title: Employers use your personal data to figure out the lowest salary you'll accept

btc.headline: Bitcoin bounces to $69K as geopolitical tension eases
btc.body:
Bitcoin reclaimed $69,000 on Monday as ceasefire talks and a delayed Trump deadline on Iran lifted risk appetite across crypto markets. Short sellers got caught offside, with forced liquidations amplifying the move higher. XRP drifted up to $1.33 but remains stuck in a narrow range, suggesting broader conviction is still thin.

The rally has a fragile foundation. One analyst warned that Bitcoin faces a potential collapse to $10,000 unless prices can reclaim $75,000 and hold it. Polymarket odds of a US invasion of Iran hit 63% before the platform pulled related prediction markets after user backlash - a sign that geopolitical risk is far from priced out.

sources.title: XRP drifts higher to $1.33, but range-bound trade still dominates
Bitcoin reclaims $69,000 as ceasefire talks surface and crypto shorts get squeezed
Bitcoin meltdown to $10,000 remains likely unless prices reclaim $75,000, analyst says
Trump-Iran deadline chaos sends crypto higher while cease-fire hopes rise
Polymarket odds of US invading Iran this year reach 63% after Trump's post
Polymarket pulls controversial Iran rescue markets after intense backlash

btc.headline: Saylor hints at fresh Bitcoin buy after Strategy's pause
btc.body:
Michael Saylor posted his usual Monday tracker graphic, signaling Strategy is preparing another Bitcoin purchase after a week without buying. The company has used equity and debt raises to fund a near-continuous accumulation strategy, making any pause notable. Markets read the hint as bullish, adding fuel to Monday's price recovery.

Strategy remains the largest corporate holder of Bitcoin, and Saylor's public telegraphing of buys has become a market-moving ritual in itself.

sources.title: Michael Saylor hints at next Strategy Bitcoin buy after weeklong pause

btc.headline: China bans Bitchat, Rwanda blocks Bybit P2P trading
btc.body:
Apple removed Jack Dorsey's Bitchat app from its China App Store at Beijing's request, the latest casualty of China's tight grip on decentralized communication tools. Bitchat uses Bitcoin's Nostr protocol and had positioned itself as a censorship-resistant messaging platform - exactly the kind of product Beijing moves to suppress.

Separately, Rwanda ordered Bybit to shut down its peer-to-peer platform that allowed franc-to-crypto trading, citing unlicensed operations. The two actions, on opposite sides of the world, underscore how regulators continue to squeeze crypto's on-ramps and infrastructure.

sources.title: Apple pulls Jack Dorsey's Bitchat from China at Beijing’s request
Rwanda swats Bybit’s P2P platform offering franc-to-crypto trading

btc.headline: Quantum computing threat to Bitcoin explained - and it's real
btc.body:
A new explainer from CoinDesk breaks down why quantum computing poses a genuine long-term threat to Bitcoin's cryptographic security. Current quantum machines are nowhere near powerful enough to crack Bitcoin's encryption, but the trajectory of the technology is what concerns researchers. The threat is not imminent. It is, however, directional.

Meanwhile, a legal dispute around the Drift protocol is drawing attention after a crypto attorney said the incident could meet the threshold for civil negligence - a reminder that on-chain incidents increasingly carry real-world legal consequences.

sources.title: Crypto attorney says Drift incident may qualify as 'civil negligence'
A simple explainer on what quantum computing actually is, and why it is terrifying for bitcoin

food.name: Miso-Glazed Black Cod
food.description: Silky, caramelised white fish marinated in sweet miso and sake, broiled until lacquered and tender
food.tip: Marinate the cod for at least 24 hours — the longer the miso sits on the fish, the deeper the umami crust when it hits the heat

exercise.name: Monday Reset: Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.description: Cycle through farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead carries for 40-metre lengths, resting 90 seconds between sets — builds grip, core stability, and posture in a way machines simply cannot replicate

learn.description: A hand-cranked Greek analogue computer from roughly 100 BCE, recovered from a shipwreck in 1901, capable of predicting solar and lunar eclipses, planetary positions, and the timing of the Olympic Games with extraordinary precision
learn.why: Recent X-ray tomography has revealed gearing complexity that wasn't thought possible for another 1,400 years — spending an hour with it fundamentally destabilises any comfortable assumption you hold about ancient technological capability
